The width of a frame is proportional to its CPU time, not call depth, which is counterintuitive to engineers reading them for the first time.

children.title: Garbage Collection Analysis
children.description: JVM GC pauses cause stop-the-world latency spikes that appear as periodic high p99 values in load test results; without GC logs, these spikes look like application bugs. Enabling GC logging and analyzing it with GCViewer or GCEasy reveals pause frequency, duration, and heap pressure.

children.title: Heap Dump Analysis
children.description: Memory leaks in long-running services only become visible during soak tests; a heap dump captured at the peak reveals which objects are retained and which classes are responsible. Without heap dump analysis skills, memory leaks are addressed by increasing heap size rather than fixing the root cause.

performance.title: Stress, Spike, and Soak Testing
performance.description: Design specialized test scenarios beyond simple load tests to validate system resilience under extreme conditions. Learn what to measure and when to stop each test type.
topics: Stress testing to find the breaking point
Defining pass/fail criteria per test type
Capacity planning from stress test data

resources.label: Stress vs Load vs Soak Testing

performance.challenge: Design and execute a soak test running for 2 hours, monitor for memory leaks and error rate degradation, and document findings with Grafana screenshots.

children.title: Finding the Breaking Point
children.description: Stress testing increments load beyond expected peak until the system degrades or fails, revealing the true capacity ceiling and failure mode. Without this knowledge, capacity planning relies on guesswork and systems fail in production at loads that were never tested.

children.title: System Recovery Validation
children.description: A system that degrades gracefully under stress but never recovers is as dangerous as one that crashes; validating auto-recovery after stress test completion is a distinct and mandatory test assertion. Recovery time objectives (RTOs) should be defined before the stress test begins.

children.title: Spike Test Design
children.description: Spike tests simulate sudden traffic bursts — flash sales, viral events, or DDoS attacks — that neither load nor stress tests model. A system that handles 10x normal traffic gracefully when gradually ramped may fail completely when the same load arrives within 30 seconds.

children.title: Defining Pass/Fail Criteria per Test Type
children.description: Applying load test pass/fail criteria to a stress test will always produce a failure, since stress tests are designed to exceed normal operating limits. Each test type needs its own thresholds: error rate tolerance for spike tests is higher than for soak tests.

children.title: Soak Test Duration and Monitoring
children.description: Memory leaks and connection pool exhaustion are invisible in short tests because they accumulate slowly; soak tests run at moderate load for 2-24 hours specifically to surface time-dependent degradation. Monitoring memory and error rate trends rather than just instantaneous values is the only way to detect a leak.

resources.label: Memory Leak Detection via Soak Tests

children.title: Capacity Planning from Test Data
children.description: Stress test results translate directly into horizontal scaling rules: knowing the RPS at which error rate exceeds 1% tells you exactly when to add nodes. Without this data, autoscaling thresholds are set by intuition rather than evidence, leading to both over-provisioning and unexpected capacity failures.

children.title: Breakpoint Testing
children.description: Breakpoint testing automates the process of incrementally increasing load until a target metric crosses a threshold, removing the need for manual binary-search style runs. This produces a precise capacity number rather than a range, improving infrastructure cost modeling accuracy.

children.title: Chaos Engineering Overlap with Performance
children.description: Chaos engineering injects failures during load tests to validate that performance SLOs hold even when individual components fail; this is the intersection where resilience and performance testing meet. Teams that treat the two disciplines as separate miss the most production-realistic scenarios.

performance.title: API and Microservices Performance Testing
performance.description: Test performance at the API and microservice layer independently of the UI to get faster, more targeted results. Learn distributed tracing concepts to follow requests across services.
performance.challenge: Write k6 tests that independently load-test 3 microservices, compare their p99 latencies, and correlate with distributed traces for the slowest endpoint.

children.title: k6 in GitHub Actions
children.description: Running performance tests in CI ensures that no code change degrades response times without immediate visibility; without CI integration, performance tests run sporadically and regressions accumulate undetected across many releases. GitHub Actions' matrix strategy enables parallel execution across multiple test scenarios.

children.title: Automated Threshold-Based Pass/Fail in CI
children.description: A performance test that always exits with code 0 cannot block a PR, making CI integration meaningless; k6 thresholds that exit with code 99 on breach provide the CI gate that enforces SLOs. Every CI performance job must define exit-code-sensitive pipeline conditions.

children.title: Containerized k6 Test Environments
children.description: Running k6 from a Docker container guarantees identical tool versions and network conditions across every CI runner, eliminating "works on my machine" inconsistencies. The official k6 Docker image requires no installation step and integrates cleanly into existing Docker-based pipelines.

children.title: Docker Compose for Full Stack Performance Testing
children.description: Testing a microservice in isolation ignores the performance impact of its downstream dependencies; Docker Compose brings up the entire stack in CI with a single command, enabling realistic integration-level performance tests. Service health checks in the Compose file prevent tests from starting before dependencies are ready.

children.title: Nightly Performance Regression Runs
children.description: Smoke-level performance tests on every PR are fast but shallow; nightly soak and load tests against a staging environment catch regressions that only appear under sustained traffic. Separating nightly jobs from PR jobs by test depth prevents CI from becoming too slow to use.

children.title: Alerting on Performance Regressions in CI
children.description: A failed CI performance job that sends no notification relies on engineers actively checking pipeline status; Slack or email alerts on threshold breaches ensure immediate response before the regression reaches production. Alert messages should include the specific threshold breached and a direct link to the Grafana dashboard.

children.title: Performance Trend Dashboards for CI
children.description: Point-in-time test results cannot answer whether this release is faster or slower than last week; trend dashboards plotting p95 latency and error rate per Git commit make regressions and improvements immediately visible to the team. k6 Cloud and self-hosted InfluxDB both support this pattern.

children.title: Flakiness Detection in Performance Tests
children.description: A performance test that sometimes passes and sometimes fails at the same load level is providing noise, not signal, and will eventually be ignored or disabled by the team. Statistical variance analysis over multiple runs distinguishes genuine regressions from natural measurement fluctuation.

performance.title: Performance Test Reporting and SLA Definition
performance.description: Translate raw performance numbers into actionable insights for development teams and management. Learn to define SLAs, write performance test reports, and track trends over releases.
topics: Defining SLAs and SLOs for APIs

resources.label: SLA vs SLO vs SLI Guide

performance.challenge: Write a complete performance test report comparing two releases, including trend charts, SLA compliance table, and a recommendation section.

children.title: API-Level vs UI-Level Performance Testing
children.description: UI-level performance tests are 5-10x slower to execute than API-level tests and introduce browser rendering variability that obscures server-side performance signals. Testing at the API layer first isolates backend performance from frontend concerns and produces faster, more actionable feedback.

children.title: Isolating Microservice Performance
children.description: Testing a microservice through an API gateway conflates the gateway's latency with the service's latency, making it impossible to identify which component is degraded. Direct service testing with controlled request rates isolates each service's true performance characteristics.

children.title: Distributed Tracing with Jaeger
children.description: In a microservices architecture, a slow end-to-end response time is caused by exactly one span in one service; distributed tracing finds that span in seconds while manual log correlation would take hours. Jaeger provides the UI to visualize span waterfalls and compare slow traces against fast baselines.

children.title: OpenTelemetry for Performance Context
children.description: OpenTelemetry provides a vendor-neutral standard for emitting traces, metrics, and logs from any language; instrumenting services with OTel means you can switch backends from Jaeger to Tempo without rewriting instrumentation. Without tracing context, performance problems in service mesh architectures are nearly impossible to diagnose.

children.title: Performance Testing in Docker Environments
children.description: Containerized services have different networking characteristics than bare-metal deployments, including overlay network latency and container resource limits that act as artificial ceilings. Performance test environments must match production container resource constraints to produce valid results.

children.title: Service Mesh Performance Considerations
children.description: Service meshes like Istio add a sidecar proxy to every pod, introducing measurable latency per hop that accumulates significantly in deep call chains. Performance testers who ignore the mesh underestimate end-to-end latency in production and miscalibrate SLO thresholds.

children.title: Consumer-Driven Contract Performance Checks
children.description: Consumer contracts typically define functional expectations but rarely include latency constraints; adding performance assertions to Pact contracts prevents providers from shipping functional changes that silently degrade response time. This bridges the gap between contract testing and performance testing disciplines.

children.title: Integration Performance Test Design
children.description: Integration performance tests target the seams between services rather than individual endpoints, revealing cascade effects where one slow downstream call doubles the latency of multiple upstream services. Identifying these amplification points is only possible with cross-service instrumentation.

performance.title: CI/CD Integration and Continuous Performance Testing
performance.description: Embed performance tests into your CI/CD pipeline so every deployment is automatically validated against performance thresholds. Block releases that violate SLA automatically.
performance.challenge: Configure a GitHub Actions workflow that runs k6 smoke and load tests on every PR, fails the build if p95 exceeds threshold, and posts a Grafana snapshot as a comment.

children.title: Defining SLAs and SLOs for APIs
children.description: SLOs without SLAs are aspirational targets with no accountability; SLAs without SLOs have no early warning system before contractual breach. Establishing both in terms of p95 latency and error rate per endpoint creates a two-tier system that gives the team time to react before customers are impacted.

children.title: Performance Baseline Establishment
children.description: A performance report that says "p95 latency is 450ms" is only actionable when compared against a baseline; without the baseline, stakeholders cannot determine if 450ms represents improvement, regression, or expected behavior. Baseline runs must be conducted under identical environment conditions to be valid comparators.

children.title: Performance Trend Analysis Across Releases
children.description: Single-run comparisons between releases miss gradual regressions that accumulate across many small commits; trend charts over 10+ runs reveal slow drift before it becomes a production incident. Storing all test results in InfluxDB or a time-series store is a prerequisite for meaningful trend analysis.

children.title: Grafana Annotations for Test Events
children.description: Annotations create visual markers on Grafana timelines that align load test phases with metric changes, making it immediately clear that a latency increase started precisely when the load ramped to 500 VUs. Without annotations, correlating test events with metric changes requires manual timestamp arithmetic.

children.title: Executive Performance Test Reports
children.description: Technical engineers and business stakeholders need fundamentally different information from the same test run; an executive summary with pass/fail and business impact should accompany every detailed technical report. Reports that bury the conclusion in raw data tables go unread and unacted upon.

children.title: SLA Compliance Tables
children.description: SLA compliance tables compare each measured endpoint percentile against its defined threshold, producing a single at-a-glance pass/fail matrix that stakeholders can sign off on. Without this structure, performance reports invite endless discussion about which numbers matter instead of focusing on the binary compliance question.

children.title: Structured Recommendation Format
children.description: Performance recommendations without priority and effort estimates are wish lists, not action plans; ranking findings by performance impact versus implementation cost gives development teams a clear starting point. Each recommendation should include the specific metric improved, the expected gain, and the implementation risk.

children.title: Test Result Archiving Strategy
children.description: Without an archiving strategy, old test results are overwritten or lost, making baseline comparisons and trend analysis impossible months after the tests ran. Naming conventions that include the build number, environment, and timestamp in every result file enable instant retrieval for any historical comparison.

security-qa.title: Web Security Fundamentals for Testers
security-qa.description: Build a mental model of web application security by studying the OWASP Top 10 and common vulnerability classes. Learn how HTTP, cookies, and sessions work from an attacker perspective.
topics: Attacker mindset and threat modeling intro
security-qa.challenge: Map all OWASP Top 10 items to a sample application and document which attack surfaces exist for each category.

children.title: OWASP Top 10
children.description: The OWASP Top 10 is the industry-standard baseline for web application risk. Without knowing which vulnerability classes matter most, a tester cannot prioritize effort and will miss the defects that cause real breaches.

children.title: HTTP, Cookies, and Sessions
children.description: Every web vulnerability travels through HTTP. Misunderstanding how cookies store state or how session tokens are issued leads to missed authentication and session-fixation bugs that attackers exploit trivially.

children.title: Intercepting Proxies
children.description: An intercepting proxy lets you pause, inspect, and modify live HTTP traffic between browser and server. Without this capability you cannot observe the full request/response cycle that reveals hidden parameters and insecure headers.

children.title: Same-Origin Policy and CORS
children.description: CORS misconfiguration is one of the most common API vulnerabilities. If a tester cannot recognize when a server returns overly permissive Access-Control headers, entire classes of cross-origin data-theft bugs go unreported.

children.title: Attacker Mindset
children.description: Security testing requires reasoning about what an adversary wants to achieve, not just what the system is supposed to do. Testers who only think in happy paths consistently miss privilege-escalation and data-exfiltration scenarios.

children.title: TLS and Certificate Basics
children.description: Weak TLS configurations silently expose data in transit. Understanding certificate validation, cipher suites, and HSTS means testers can flag insecure transport-layer settings before they reach production.

resources.label: OWASP Transport Layer Security Cheat Sheet

children.title: Deliberately Vulnerable Apps
children.description: Practicing on real targets is illegal. Deliberately vulnerable applications like DVWA and Juice Shop provide a safe, legal environment to develop and verify attack techniques before applying them to actual test targets.

children.title: PortSwigger Web Security Academy
children.description: Free, structured labs tied directly to every OWASP vulnerability class. Without hands-on labs, theoretical knowledge does not transfer to real-world bug detection and reporting.

security-qa.title: Burp Suite for Manual Security Testing
security-qa.description: Use Burp Suite Community Edition as an intercepting proxy to manually inspect, modify, and replay HTTP requests. Understand the Burp Suite workflow for manual vulnerability hunting.
security-qa.challenge: Use Burp Suite Repeater to manually test for SQL injection on a deliberately vulnerable app like DVWA and document your findings.

children.title: Proxy and Interceptor
children.description: Burp Proxy is the heart of manual security testing. Misconfiguring the proxy or not understanding scope settings causes testers to flood logs with irrelevant traffic and miss targeted request manipulation opportunities.

children.title: Repeater and Intruder
children.description: Repeater lets testers craft precise, repeatable request variations to confirm vulnerabilities, while Intruder automates payload injection for fuzzing. Without these tools, confirming an injection bug requires tedious manual re-typing.

children.title: Writing Actionable Security Findings
children.description: A vulnerability without clear reproduction steps and impact evidence gets deprioritized or disputed. Security testers who cannot write structured findings waste developer time and delay remediation.

children.title: Decoder and Target Site Map
children.description: Burp Decoder reveals encoding tricks attackers use to bypass input filters, and the site map builds a full picture of the application surface. Missing these tools means relying on guesswork to identify all testable endpoints.

children.title: When to Use Manual Testing
children.description: Automated scanners miss logic flaws, privilege escalation paths, and multi-step attack chains. Understanding when automation is insufficient prevents false confidence in scanner results that cover only well-known patterns.

children.title: Scoping a Security Test
children.description: Testing out-of-scope systems without authorization is a legal offense. Defining clear scope before any test begins protects both the tester and the organization, and ensures effort goes to the right targets.

children.title: Installing Burp CA Certificate
children.description: Without the Burp CA certificate installed in the browser, TLS-encrypted HTTPS traffic cannot be intercepted. Skipping this step means testers see only plaintext HTTP and miss all modern HTTPS-only security headers.

children.title: Scope and Filter Configuration
children.description: Poorly scoped Burp targets generate enormous noise and slow down the proxy. Correct include/exclude filters let the tester focus on the application under test and keep the site map clean for analysis.

security-qa.title: OWASP ZAP for DAST Scanning
security-qa.description: Automate security scanning using OWASP ZAP as a Dynamic Application Security Testing tool. Learn to run passive and active scans and interpret ZAP reports.
topics: ZAP alerts: risk and confidence levels
security-qa.challenge: Run a full ZAP active scan against a deliberately vulnerable app, triage all high and medium alerts, and write a vulnerability report.

children.title: Passive vs Active Scanning
children.description: Passive scanning observes traffic without sending attack payloads, making it safe for production environments; active scanning fires real attack probes. Confusing the two can crash a production application or generate false alert fatigue.

children.title: ZAP Alert Risk Levels
children.description: ZAP classifies alerts by risk (High/Medium/Low/Informational) and confidence. Misinterpreting a Low-confidence High-risk alert as confirmed leads to wasted developer effort on false positives and erodes trust in security testing.

children.title: ZAP Daemon and API
children.description: ZAP's headless daemon mode and REST API allow scans to be scripted and triggered without a GUI, which is essential for CI/CD integration. Without this, security scanning stays a manual step that gets skipped under release pressure.

children.title: Spider and AJAX Spider
children.description: The traditional spider misses Single Page Application routes that are only reachable through JavaScript. Using the AJAX spider for modern SPAs ensures the scan covers the full application surface rather than a fraction of it.

children.title: ZAP HTML Reports
children.description: Communicating scan results to developers and management requires a structured report, not a raw alert dump. ZAP's built-in HTML and XML reporters provide the organized evidence needed for ticket creation and compliance documentation.

children.title: Authenticated Scanning
children.description: An unauthenticated ZAP scan misses all vulnerabilities behind the login wall, which is where the most sensitive functionality lives. Setting up session handling and form-based authentication is mandatory for complete coverage.

children.title: Complementary Scanning Approaches
children.description: DAST finds runtime vulnerabilities in running applications while SAST analyzes source code at rest. Relying on only one type produces large blind spots: SAST misses configuration issues and DAST misses insecure coding patterns.

children.title: False Positive Triage
children.description: Automated scanners routinely generate false positives that, if reported unchecked, destroy developer confidence in security testing. Every automated finding must be manually confirmed before it enters a bug tracker.

security-qa.title: Injection Vulnerabilities: SQL, XSS, Command
security-qa.description: Deep-dive into the most impactful injection vulnerability classes including SQL injection, Cross-Site Scripting, and OS command injection. Practice in safe lab environments.
topics: SQL injection types: classic, blind, time-based
security-qa.challenge: Complete all PortSwigger Web Security Academy SQL injection and XSS labs at the apprentice level and document each exploit technique used.

children.title: SQL Injection
children.description: SQL injection remains the leading cause of large-scale data breaches because it allows complete database extraction or destruction with a single crafted input. Testers who cannot identify classic, blind, and time-based variants miss confirmed exploitable vulnerabilities.

children.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
children.description: XSS allows attackers to inject scripts into pages viewed by other users, enabling session theft and phishing. Distinguishing reflected, stored, and DOM-based XSS is necessary because each requires a different detection and remediation approach.

children.title: SQLMap
children.description: SQLMap automates SQL injection detection and exploitation, saving hours of manual parameter fuzzing. Without knowing its capabilities and limitations, testers either re-invent the wheel manually or run it blindly and miss corner cases.

children.title: CSRF Fundamentals
children.description: CSRF forces authenticated users to unknowingly execute state-changing requests. Testers who overlook CSRF allow attackers to perform account changes, fund transfers, and admin actions on behalf of logged-in victims.

children.title: Encoding and Escaping
children.description: Injection vulnerabilities exist because user input is interpreted as executable code. Understanding the difference between encoding (output escaping) and validation (input filtering) is essential to correctly classifying whether a fix actually remediates the root cause.

children.title: OS Command Injection
children.description: When applications pass user input to shell commands without sanitization, attackers gain full server access. This class is easily overlooked because it appears in unexpected places like file upload filename handling and ping utilities.

children.title: Content Security Policy Testing
children.description: CSP is the primary browser-side defense against XSS. A tester who cannot read and evaluate a CSP header cannot confirm whether an XSS mitigation is actually effective or trivially bypassable.

children.title: Payload Bypass Techniques
children.description: WAFs and input filters block obvious payloads but miss encoded or case-varied variants. Understanding common bypass techniques allows testers to distinguish a true fix from a superficial block that any real attacker would circumvent.

security-qa.title: Authentication and Authorization Testing
security-qa.description: Test authentication mechanisms for weaknesses including brute force susceptibility, session fixation, and insecure token handling. Validate authorization controls to prevent privilege escalation.
topics: Brute force and account lockout testing
security-qa.challenge: Test a demo app for IDOR vulnerabilities and broken authentication, document at least 3 findings with CVSS scores and remediation advice.

children.title: Brute Force and Account Lockout
children.description: Absent account lockout, an attacker with a credential list can eventually log into any account. Testers who do not verify lockout thresholds and CAPTCHA presence leave the most basic authentication protection unverified.

children.title: JWT Security Testing
children.description: JWT vulnerabilities including algorithm confusion (none attack, RS256 to HS256 swap) and weak secrets allow full authentication bypass. Testers who treat JWTs as opaque strings miss the most impactful token-level attacks.

children.title: IDOR Detection
children.description: Insecure Direct Object References allow users to access other users' data simply by changing an ID in a URL or request body. IDOR is the most commonly reported high-severity bug class on bug bounty platforms and is entirely missed by automated scanners.

children.title: Privilege Escalation Testing
children.description: Vertical escalation lets a normal user perform admin actions; horizontal escalation lets one user act on another user's account. Both require mapping the full role matrix and deliberately testing every action-role combination.

children.title: Session Token Entropy
children.description: Predictable session tokens can be guessed or brute-forced to hijack active user sessions. Verifying token length, randomness, and invalidation on logout is a mandatory part of any authentication security review.

children.title: OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect Security
children.description: OAuth misconfigurations including open redirectors, state parameter omission, and implicit flow weaknesses allow attackers to steal tokens and impersonate users. Testers unfamiliar with these flows cannot verify the most critical modern SSO integrations.

children.title: Scoring Vulnerabilities with CVSS v3
children.description: CVSS scores translate technical vulnerability details into a risk number that drives remediation priority. Without accurate CVSS scoring, security findings get triaged based on gut feeling, and critical issues are routinely deferred.

children.title: Remediation Verification
children.description: A closed vulnerability ticket does not mean the fix is correct. Re-testing after remediation to confirm the specific attack vector is blocked—and that no regression was introduced—is the final step that closes the security testing loop.

security-qa.title: Security Test Automation with Selenium
security-qa.description: Combine functional UI automation with security scanning by running ZAP as a proxy behind Selenium tests. Detect security issues that only appear during authenticated user flows.
topics: Integrating ZAP alerts into test results
security-qa.challenge: Configure Selenium to route through ZAP during a full login and checkout flow, then extract and report all medium+ alerts found during the run.

children.title: Proxy Chaining for Authenticated Scans
children.description: Running Selenium through ZAP as a proxy allows security scanning of pages that require login, dynamically loaded content, and multi-step workflows. Without this setup, automated DAST misses the most sensitive and high-risk application areas.

children.title: Passive Scanning During Functional Tests
children.description: Passive scanning during a functional test run costs zero extra time and adds free security coverage. Teams that skip this miss XSS reflections, missing security headers, and information disclosure bugs that appear only in authenticated flows.

children.title: Security Response Header Assertions
children.description: Security headers like HSTS, X-Frame-Options, and X-Content-Type-Options are the last line of defense against many client-side attacks. Asserting their presence in automated tests prevents header regressions from shipping silently.

resources.label: OWASP HTTP Security Headers Cheat Sheet

children.title: Integrating ZAP Alerts into Pytest
children.description: Failing a Pytest run when ZAP finds high-severity alerts enforces a security gate without a separate pipeline step. Without this integration, scan results live in separate reports that no one reviews after a sprint ends.

children.title: Secrets Management in Test Code
children.description: Test suites that hard-code credentials or API keys in source code expose those secrets to anyone with repository access. Using environment variables and secret stores prevents credentials from leaking into version control history.

children.title: Test Data Isolation
children.description: Security tests that share state with functional tests risk polluting the environment or exposing production data. Isolated test accounts, sandboxed environments, and teardown procedures ensure security tests do not cause side effects.

children.title: Clickjacking Detection
children.description: Clickjacking embeds a target page in an invisible iframe to trick users into clicking hidden buttons. Automated functional tests never check for it, making an explicit X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors assertion the only reliable detection method.

children.title: Open Redirect Testing
children.description: Open redirects in login flows are used as phishing launchers that carry the trust of the target domain. They appear innocent in functional tests because the redirect succeeds—only a security-aware assertion on the destination URL catches them.

security-qa.title: API Security Testing
security-qa.description: Apply OWASP API Security Top 10 guidance to systematically test REST APIs for vulnerabilities specific to API endpoints. Focus on broken object-level auth and mass assignment.
security-qa.challenge: Test a vulnerable API lab for all OWASP API Top 10 categories, document findings with request/response evidence and CVSS v3 scores.

children.title: OWASP API Security Top 10
children.description: API vulnerabilities differ from web app vulnerabilities because APIs expose raw business logic without a UI acting as a filter. The OWASP API Security Top 10 codifies the specific risk categories that automated and manual API tests must cover.

children.title: Broken Object Level Authorization
children.description: BOLA (IDOR at the API layer) is the number-one API vulnerability class. It occurs when an API endpoint uses caller-supplied IDs without verifying the caller owns the requested resource, granting access to any object by changing a single parameter.

children.title: Mass Assignment
children.description: Mass assignment vulnerabilities arise when APIs bind all request body fields to model properties without an allowlist, letting attackers set admin flags or balance fields. Without explicit testing of each writable field, this class is invisible to functional QA.

resources.label: OWASP API6:2023 Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows

children.title: Rate Limiting and Throttling Bypass
children.description: APIs without rate limiting are vulnerable to credential stuffing, scraping, and denial-of-service at the business logic layer. Testing for the absence of throttling is as important as testing for functional correctness.

children.title: Burp Suite for API Testing
children.description: Burp Suite's Repeater and Scanner work as effectively on JSON APIs as on HTML pages. Setting up a Postman collection as a Burp target map gives testers a structured way to fuzz every API endpoint rather than testing ad hoc.

children.title: GraphQL Security Testing
children.description: GraphQL's introspection feature can expose the complete API schema to attackers. Batch queries and deeply nested operations can also be used to extract data or cause denial of service in ways REST endpoints cannot.

children.title: OpenAPI Spec Security Review
children.description: An OpenAPI specification reveals all endpoints, parameters, authentication schemes, and response schemas. Reviewing the spec before testing identifies high-value targets and gaps between documented and undocumented endpoints.

children.title: API Versioning Security Issues
children.description: Old API versions that are left running after deprecation often lack the security controls added to newer versions. Testers who only test the current API version miss retired endpoints that attackers actively target.

security-qa.title: Security Regression and Compliance Testing
security-qa.description: Ensure that previously identified and fixed vulnerabilities do not reappear by building a security regression suite. Map tests to compliance frameworks like PCI-DSS or SOC 2.
security-qa.challenge: Build a ZAP regression scan script that checks 10 previously fixed vulnerabilities and generates a compliance-mapped report.

children.title: Vulnerability Re-test Procedures
children.description: A fix that resolves the reported payload but not the root cause will regress under a slightly different attack vector. Structured re-test procedures that test the fix, adjacent parameters, and original payload variants catch incomplete remediations.

children.title: OWASP ASVS Test Coverage Mapping
children.description: The Application Security Verification Standard provides a numbered checklist of security controls that maps directly to test cases. Without this mapping, security regression suites drift and no one can prove which controls are actually verified.

resources.label: ASVS to Test Case Mapping Guide

children.title: PCI DSS Security Testing Requirements
children.description: PCI DSS Requirement 11 mandates regular penetration testing and vulnerability scanning for any system that handles cardholder data. Teams that do not understand which controls require testing face audit failures and potential loss of payment processing ability.

children.title: SOC 2 Security Controls Testing
children.description: SOC 2 Type II audits require evidence that security controls operated continuously throughout the audit period. QA teams that understand which controls need test evidence can produce it systematically rather than scrambling during audit preparation.

children.title: Compliance Evidence Collection
children.description: Auditors require timestamped, reproducible evidence that security controls were tested and passed. Test execution logs, scan reports, and signed-off checklists are the artifacts that turn a test run into a defensible compliance record.

children.title: Traceability Between Tests and Requirements
children.description: Without traceability from each security test case to a specific compliance requirement or ASVS control, it is impossible to determine which requirements are tested and which have gaps. This traceability matrix is the core artifact of a mature security QA process.

children.title: Automated Security Regression in CI
children.description: Running a security regression suite in CI means compliance controls are validated on every code change, not just at audit time. Teams that only test at release cadence accumulate compliance debt that becomes expensive to resolve before an audit.

children.title: Security Test Reporting Cadence
children.description: Compliance requires demonstrating that testing was performed on a defined schedule. Establishing and documenting a regular reporting cadence creates an audit trail that proves continuous control monitoring rather than point-in-time testing.

security-qa.title: Threat Modeling and Security Test Planning
security-qa.description: Proactively identify security risks during design by applying threat modeling frameworks. Translate threat model outputs into actionable security test cases.
topics: Data flow diagrams for threat modeling
Security test case derivation from threats
Threat model review in agile sprints
security-qa.challenge: Create a STRIDE threat model for a login and payment feature using Threat Dragon and derive at least 15 security test cases from the model.

children.title: STRIDE Framework
children.description: STRIDE categorizes threats into Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, and Elevation of Privilege. Without a structured framework, threat modeling sessions produce inconsistent outputs that miss entire threat categories.

children.title: Data Flow Diagrams for Threat Modeling
children.description: DFDs show how data moves between users, processes, and data stores, and identify trust boundaries where attacks occur. Threat models built without DFDs are speculative and miss attack paths that only become visible when data flow is mapped explicitly.

children.title: Abuse Cases from Threat Models
children.description: Each threat identified in a STRIDE model corresponds to one or more abuse cases that QA must validate. Skipping this derivation step means the threat model produces documentation that never influences the actual test suite.

children.title: Threat Modeling in Agile Sprints
children.description: A threat model created once at project start becomes stale as features change. Running lightweight threat modeling as part of sprint refinement keeps the security test suite aligned with the current application design.

children.title: Constructing Attack Trees
children.description: Attack trees enumerate all paths an attacker could take to achieve a goal, making implicit assumptions explicit. Without attack trees, testers cover only obvious attack paths and miss chained or multi-step scenarios.

children.title: MITRE ATT&CK for Web Applications
children.description: MITRE ATT&CK catalogs real-world adversary tactics and techniques. Mapping a threat model to ATT&CK ensures that test cases cover techniques that actual threat actors use rather than only textbook vulnerabilities.

children.title: OWASP Risk Rating Methodology
children.description: OWASP's risk rating methodology combines likelihood and business impact factors to produce a prioritized risk score. Teams that skip formal risk rating spend equal effort on trivial and critical findings, failing to protect what matters most.

children.title: Security Backlog Integration
children.description: Threat model outputs that are not imported into the sprint backlog as testable stories are ignored. Translating each identified risk into a Jira ticket with acceptance criteria ensures threats drive actual test coverage, not just slide decks.

resources.label: Jira Security Issue Tracking Best Practices

security-qa.title: CI/CD DAST Integration and Secure SDLC
security-qa.description: Embed DAST scanning into CI/CD pipelines so security tests run automatically on every deployment. Champion a shift-left security culture by integrating security gates early in the SDLC.
topics: ZAP Docker image in CI/CD pipelines
SAST vs DAST in a pipeline
security-qa.challenge: Build a GitHub Actions pipeline that runs ZAP DAST and Snyk dependency scanning, fails on high-severity findings, and posts a security summary as a PR comment.

children.title: ZAP Docker in Pipelines
children.description: ZAP's official Docker image makes it trivially deployable in any CI environment without a GUI install. Teams that do not containerize their DAST tooling face environment inconsistencies that make scan results unreliable across pipeline runs.

children.title: Security Gate Thresholds
children.description: A pipeline that reports security findings but never fails the build provides no enforcement. Defining explicit thresholds (e.g., fail on any High, warn on Medium) turns security scanning from an advisory into a deployment quality gate.

children.title: Software Composition Analysis with Snyk
children.description: The majority of modern application code is third-party libraries. SCA tools like Snyk compare the dependency tree against known CVE databases and alert before a vulnerable library reaches production.

children.title: OWASP Dependency Check
children.description: OWASP Dependency Check is the open-source alternative to commercial SCA tools and integrates directly with Maven, Gradle, and CI pipelines. Without dependency scanning, known Critical CVEs ship undetected inside transitive library inclusions.

children.title: Shift-Left Security
children.description: Fixing a vulnerability in design costs orders of magnitude less than fixing it post-deployment. Shift-left security embeds testing, threat modeling, and secure code review as early in the SDLC as possible to eliminate the most expensive rework.

children.title: Security Champions Program
children.description: Security QA cannot scale if it depends on a single specialist. A security champions program embeds security-aware advocates in each development team, distributing responsibility and ensuring security considerations enter every sprint.

children.title: SonarQube for Static Analysis
children.description: SonarQube detects insecure coding patterns like SQL concatenation, hardcoded secrets, and unsafe deserialization before the code is ever deployed. Without SAST in the pipeline, these patterns reach QA only as confirmed runtime vulnerabilities.

children.title: Semgrep for Custom Security Rules
children.description: Semgrep allows teams to write custom SAST rules that match organization-specific coding standards and security patterns. Without custom rules, generic SAST tools miss in-house framework misuse that represents the largest category of proprietary application vulnerabilities.

mobile-qa.title: Mobile Testing Fundamentals
mobile-qa.description: Understand the unique challenges of mobile testing across platforms, device types, and OS versions. Set up iOS and Android development environments for manual and automated testing.
topics: iOS Simulator and Android Emulator setup
Mobile-specific test types: gesture, orientation, interrupt
mobile-qa.challenge: Perform a manual test session on a demo Android app covering gestures, orientation changes, and interrupt scenarios, documenting all defects found.

children.title: Android vs iOS Architecture
children.description: Android and iOS differ in sandboxing models, permission systems, and release cadences. Testers who conflate the two miss platform-specific crashes, permission prompts that never surface on the other OS, and OS-version fragmentation that only Android exhibits.

children.title: ADB and Device Communication
children.description: ADB is the gateway to Android devices and emulators for log capture, shell commands, file transfer, and port forwarding. Without ADB fluency, testers cannot inspect crash logs in real time, sideload test builds, or script device setup steps reliably.

children.title: Emulator vs Real Device Trade-offs
children.description: Emulators accelerate early testing and cost nothing, but they cannot replicate sensors, true battery drain, carrier-level network drops, or hardware-specific rendering bugs. Shipping without real-device validation risks defects that emulators structurally cannot expose.

children.title: Mobile-Specific Test Types
children.description: Gesture testing (swipe, pinch, long-press), orientation changes, and interrupt scenarios (incoming calls, low-battery alerts) are exclusive to mobile and invisible to desktop-web QA processes. Skipping them leaves entire interaction surfaces untested.

children.title: Mobile SDLC and Release Cycles
children.description: Mobile apps ship through App Store and Play Store review pipelines that add lead time unavailable in web deployments. Testers who ignore this context miss the earlier cut-off dates for regression testing and the impact of rejected builds on release schedules.

children.title: Defect Reporting on Mobile
children.description: Mobile bug reports require device model, OS version, build number, and a screen recording in addition to steps to reproduce. Reports missing this context are unactionable because developers cannot distinguish device-specific from universal defects.

children.title: Android Studio Testing Tools
children.description: Android Studio bundles the emulator manager, Logcat, layout inspector, and test runner in one IDE. Testers who bypass this tooling lack visibility into component hierarchy, live logs during test runs, and the ability to reproduce device configurations on demand.

children.title: Xcode and iOS Simulator
children.description: Xcode is the sole path to building and running iOS tests; no third-party tool can bypass it for native test execution on Apple silicon. Misunderstanding Xcode schemes and test plans leads to wrong targets being tested or simulator configurations not matching production.

mobile-qa.title: Appium Setup and Basic Automation
mobile-qa.description: Install and configure Appium to automate mobile apps on Android using Python. Understand Desired Capabilities and how Appium communicates with devices.
mobile-qa.challenge: Write an Appium Python script that launches a demo Android app, fills a login form, taps submit, and asserts the home screen appears.

children.title: Appium Server and WebDriver Protocol
children.description: Appium translates WebDriver JSON Wire Protocol commands into native UIAutomator2 (Android) or XCUITest (iOS) calls. Misunderstanding this relay means testers cannot diagnose session startup failures, capability mismatches, or port conflicts that silently drop test sessions.

children.title: Desired Capabilities and W3C Caps
children.description: Desired Capabilities (now W3C options) tell Appium which device, OS version, app path, and automation driver to use. A single misconfigured capability such as wrong platformVersion or missing appPackage causes the entire session to refuse to start with an opaque error.

children.title: Appium Inspector for Element Discovery
children.description: Appium Inspector renders the live UI accessibility tree so testers can extract resource-id, content-desc, and XPath locators without guessing. Skipping this step leads to brittle XPath locators that break on every layout change instead of stable resource-ids.

children.title: Touch Interactions and W3C Actions API
children.description: Mobile automation requires the W3C Actions API for multi-touch gestures because the older TouchAction class is deprecated and removed in Appium 2. Tests that still use legacy APIs will break silently or throw NotImplementedError on Appium 2 drivers.

children.title: Appium-Python-Client Setup
children.description: The Appium Python client wraps Selenium's WebDriver with mobile-specific extensions like shake, lock, and hide-keyboard. Forgetting to install the correct client version against the Appium server version causes method-not-found errors that masquerade as server bugs.

children.title: Session Management and Teardown
children.description: Appium sessions that are not explicitly quit leave the device in a locked state, preventing subsequent test runs and consuming device licences on cloud farms. Proper fixture teardown with driver.quit() in a finally block is mandatory for reliable CI runs.

children.title: Login Flow Automation Pattern
children.description: The login flow is the canonical first Appium test because it exercises capability negotiation, element finding, text input, tap, and assertion in one script. Getting this wrong exposes capability errors, keyboard overlay issues, and implicit wait timing problems all at once.

children.title: Explicit Waits vs Implicit Waits
children.description: Implicit waits apply globally and interact badly with explicit waits, causing unpredictable timeout behavior. On mobile, where network-dependent UI animations are slow, explicit WebDriverWait with expected conditions is the only reliable synchronization strategy.

mobile-qa.title: XCTest for iOS Native Testing
mobile-qa.description: Use Apple XCTest framework to write native UI tests for iOS applications in Swift. Learn how XCTest integrates with Xcode and the test runner.
mobile-qa.challenge: Write an XCTest UI test suite for a sample iOS app that covers 5 critical user flows and runs green in the Xcode test runner.

children.title: XCUIApplication and Element Queries
children.description: XCUIApplication represents the app under test and is the root of all element queries in XCTest. Testers who do not understand the query predicate system write overly broad queries that match multiple elements and throw ambiguous element errors at runtime.

children.title: XCTest Assertions
children.description: XCTest provides XCTAssert variants that format failure messages and attach them to the Xcode test result bundle. Tests that use plain Swift assert() instead of XCTAssert crash the test process rather than recording a failure, hiding all remaining assertions in the suite.

children.title: Xcode Test Schemes and Plans
children.description: Xcode test plans define which tests run, in which order, with what language and region settings, and what diagnostic options are active. Teams that skip test plans cannot efficiently run subsets of tests, leading to slow full-suite runs on every CI trigger.

children.title: UI Test Recording in Xcode
children.description: Xcode can record interactions and emit XCUITest code, which is useful for discovering the correct element locators for unfamiliar views. Relying on recorded code verbatim produces fragile tests; recording should be used only for locator discovery, then replaced with readable query chains.

children.title: XCTest Performance Metrics
children.description: XCTest's measureBlock captures CPU time, memory, and clock time for repeated code paths and fails the test when a metric exceeds a defined baseline. Without this, iOS performance regressions introduced by refactors go undetected until users report slow screens.

children.title: Simulator vs Physical Device for XCTest
children.description: XCTest runs on both simulators and physical devices but some APIs like push notifications, NFC, and Camera are unavailable in the simulator. Test plans that never target a real device will pass on simulator but fail in production for any feature touching restricted hardware.

children.title: Testing with Xcode Guide
children.description: Apple's official Testing with Xcode guide covers the full lifecycle from unit to UI tests, including code coverage and test result bundles. Testers who have not read it routinely misconfigure test targets, miss coverage reports, and fail to leverage result bundle diffing for CI.

children.title: XCTest and CI Pipeline Integration
children.description: xcodebuild test produces result bundles and JUnit XML that CI systems consume for pass/fail gating and trend reporting. Teams that run tests only inside Xcode GUI cannot enforce test gates on pull requests or track test flakiness over time.

mobile-qa.title: Espresso for Android Native Testing
mobile-qa.description: Write fast, reliable Android UI tests using Google Espresso, which runs in-process for maximum speed. Learn Espresso matchers, actions, and assertions.
mobile-qa.challenge: Write an Espresso test suite for a sample Android app that tests a multi-screen flow including an async network call using IdlingResource.

children.title: Espresso Synchronization Model
children.description: Espresso automatically waits for the UI thread and AsyncTask queue to be idle before executing each action, eliminating most timing-related flakiness. Replacing Espresso with Thread.sleep() calls defeats this mechanism entirely and produces tests that are both slower and less reliable.

children.title: ViewMatchers, ViewActions, ViewAssertions
children.description: Espresso's three-part API—find with a Matcher, act with a ViewAction, check with a ViewAssertion—keeps each test step single-responsibility and produces readable failure messages. Conflating find-and-act in one call hides which step failed and makes debugging test breakages ambiguous.

children.title: IdlingResource for Async Operations
children.description: IdlingResource tells Espresso when a custom async operation such as a Retrofit network call or RxJava observable has completed so it can safely proceed. Without IdlingResource registration, tests race against network responses and produce sporadic failures that differ between fast CI machines and slow real devices.

children.title: Espresso Intents for Navigation Testing
children.description: Espresso-Intents intercepts and validates Android Intents so tests can verify that tapping a button fires the correct Intent without actually launching the target Activity. Without this, navigation tests couple to the entire navigation graph and fail whenever a downstream screen changes.

children.title: UI Automator for System-Level Testing
children.description: UI Automator 2 operates across app boundaries and can interact with system dialogs, notifications, and the settings app that Espresso cannot touch. Tests that rely only on Espresso cannot validate permission dialogs, OS-level notifications, or cross-app deep link scenarios.

children.title: Espresso Web for WebView Testing
children.description: Espresso-Web bridges native Espresso with WebDriver-style queries for hybrid apps that render web content inside a WebView. Teams without this tool are forced to switch to Appium for every screen that uses a WebView, doubling infrastructure complexity.

children.title: Firebase Test Lab for Espresso
children.description: Firebase Test Lab runs Espresso instrumentation tests on a matrix of real Google-certified devices in the cloud without managing hardware. Teams that skip it discover device-specific Espresso failures only after shipping to production on that device model.

children.title: Espresso Test Reports and Coverage
children.description: Espresso produces JUnit XML reports that CI systems parse for pass/fail counts and test-duration trends. Without structured reports, teams have no data to detect growing flakiness, identify slow tests for optimization, or track regression rates across releases.

mobile-qa.title: BrowserStack and Real Device Testing
mobile-qa.description: Run your mobile automation tests on thousands of real devices in the cloud using BrowserStack App Automate. Understand the economics and tradeoffs of real device testing.
topics: Parallel test execution on real devices
mobile-qa.challenge: Configure your Appium suite to run against 5 real Android devices on BrowserStack in parallel and compare results across device/OS combinations.

children.title: BrowserStack App Automate
children.description: BrowserStack App Automate provides access to thousands of real iOS and Android device-OS combinations without any hardware investment. Teams that test only on a handful of in-house devices miss crashes that are reproducible only on specific manufacturer ROM customizations.

children.title: Firebase Test Lab and AWS Device Farm
children.description: Firebase Test Lab and AWS Device Farm are alternative cloud device farms with different pricing models, device catalogs, and native CI integrations. Choosing the wrong provider for your budget or DevOps stack leads to either cost overruns or integration friction that delays pipeline adoption.

children.title: Parallel Test Execution on Device Grids
children.description: Running Appium tests in parallel across multiple devices requires separate Appium server ports or use of Appium server's parallel session support. Serial execution on a single device means a 5-minute suite becomes a 50-minute pipeline blocker once coverage grows to 100 tests.

children.title: Device Matrix Strategy
children.description: A device matrix defines which OS versions and screen sizes your app officially supports and which device-OS combinations run in CI. Without a documented matrix, coverage is random and critical customer segments such as Android 10 mid-range phones may never be tested.

children.title: Network Condition Simulation on BrowserStack
children.description: BrowserStack App Automate lets you simulate 2G, 3G, LTE, and airplane mode conditions to validate app behavior on degraded networks. Apps that are only tested on fast Wi-Fi exhibit loading spinners, timeouts, and cached-data staleness bugs that only manifest on mobile data.

children.title: Session Logs and Video Recording
children.description: Cloud device farms provide video recordings, Appium server logs, and device logs for every test session to aid post-failure diagnosis. Without these artifacts, debugging failures that only reproduce on specific real devices requires manual reproduction attempts that waste engineer time.

children.title: Smart Test Sharding
children.description: Sharding distributes your test suite across N devices in parallel, cutting wall-clock time by roughly N. Without sharding, large real-device suites on cloud farms accumulate per-minute costs and slow feedback loops that pressure teams to reduce test coverage instead of infrastructure.

children.title: Uploading and Managing App Builds
children.description: Cloud device farms require uploading the app binary via REST API before each test run, and stale builds cause false-positive passes against old code. Automating the upload step in CI ensures tests always run against the build that triggered the pipeline.

mobile-qa.title: Mobile Test Design: Gestures and Interrupts
mobile-qa.description: Test mobile-specific interactions including complex gestures, deep links, push notifications, and interrupt handling. Validate that apps behave correctly under real-world conditions.
topics: Implementing swipe, scroll, pinch with Appium
Incoming call and SMS interrupt testing
Background and foreground app state testing
mobile-qa.challenge: Write Appium tests that validate a card-swipe onboarding flow, a deep link launch, and graceful handling of an interrupt phone call.

children.title: Complex Gestures with W3C Actions API
children.description: Swipe, pinch, and long-press are first-class interactions in mobile apps but require the W3C PointerInput Actions API in Appium 2. Tests that only use tap and sendKeys leave entire gesture-driven navigation flows like card swipes and map zooms completely untested.

children.title: Deep Link and Push Notification Testing
children.description: Deep links and push notifications are entry points that bypass the normal app launch path and land users in specific states. Untested deep links produce crashes or empty screens for users who tap notifications, which is a high-visibility defect that appears in app store reviews.

children.title: Android Network Throttling via ADB
children.description: ADB's network emulation commands let you simulate bandwidth limits and latency on a physical device without a cloud farm. Teams without this capability cannot verify that loading states render correctly, that timeouts are not too aggressive, or that offline caching works before investing in cloud device time.

children.title: Offline and Flaky Network Testing
children.description: Mobile users frequently move between connectivity states; an app that crashes when toggling airplane mode or loses data on a dropped API call fails the most common real-world scenario. Without dedicated offline tests, these regressions reach production because functional test suites never toggle connectivity.

children.title: Incoming Call and SMS Interrupts
children.description: Incoming calls place the app in the background and require the app to restore state cleanly when the call ends. Apps that lose form data, reset navigation, or deadlock on foreground return have a defect that is structurally invisible to any test that never triggers an interrupt.

children.title: Background and Foreground State Testing
children.description: Apps must save and restore UI state when the OS kills the background process to reclaim memory, which is a normal occurrence on low-RAM devices. Tests that never backgrounded the app will pass on high-memory test devices but fail for the majority of budget Android users.

children.title: Orientation and Screen Size Testing
children.description: Layout constraints that work in portrait can break in landscape, and tablet layouts differ from phone layouts even for the same OS version. Without an explicit rotation test in every flow, orientation-related truncation, overflow, and control-hidden bugs survive to production.

children.title: Appium W3C Actions for Scroll Testing
children.description: Scroll-to-element patterns must be implemented via W3C Actions or the mobile:scroll extension because Appium does not auto-scroll to off-screen elements. Tests that assume elements are on screen without scrolling will throw NoSuchElementException on smaller screen densities.

mobile-qa.title: Mobile Performance and Battery Testing
mobile-qa.description: Evaluate mobile app performance including startup time, frame rate, memory usage, and battery drain. Use platform-native profiling tools to identify performance regressions.
mobile-qa.challenge: Profile a sample Android app during a user journey, identify one memory issue and one rendering bottleneck, and document them with profiler screenshots.

children.title: Android Profiler in Android Studio
children.description: Android Profiler provides real-time CPU, memory, network, and energy tracks during a live test session. Teams that skip profiling ship apps with memory leaks that accumulate over long sessions and battery drain bugs that appear in app store reviews rather than in test reports.

children.title: Frame Rate and Jank Detection
children.description: Android renders at 60 fps; any frame that takes longer than 16ms causes a dropped frame visible as jank. Profile GPU Rendering bars and systrace data reveal which UI components miss the frame budget, but without measuring frame rate explicitly testers cannot quantify smoothness regressions between builds.

children.title: Xcode Instruments for iOS
children.description: Xcode Instruments hosts Time Profiler, Leaks, Allocations, and Energy Log instruments in a unified timeline. Without Instruments, iOS memory leaks and CPU spikes are discovered through user crashes rather than systematic profiling, and root-cause analysis takes orders of magnitude longer.

children.title: MetricKit for Production Performance
children.description: MetricKit delivers on-device performance and power metrics to your app from real user sessions in production. Apps without MetricKit instrumentation rely entirely on crash reports and star ratings to detect performance degradation, which means they learn about issues weeks after shipping.

children.title: Battery Drain Testing Methodology
children.description: Battery drain testing requires a controlled scenario run at a fixed screen brightness and network state over a measured period, comparing drain percentage between builds. Without this methodology, a new background location polling feature or wakelocked service will drain 15% per hour and generate app store complaints before the team notices.

children.title: App Launch Time Measurement
children.description: Cold start time above 5 seconds triggers an App Not Responding alert on Android and is a top driver of 1-star reviews on iOS. ADB shell am start -W and Xcode's launch time metric in Instruments provide objective measurements that should be part of every release gate.

children.title: Memory Leak Detection on Mobile
children.description: Memory leaks on mobile are more critical than on desktop because total RAM is constrained to 2-4GB and the OS kills apps that consume too much. LeakCanary for Android and Instruments Leaks for iOS automate detection, but they must be integrated into the test cycle rather than used ad hoc.

children.title: Android Vitals and Core Metrics
children.description: Google Play Console surfaces Android Vitals including ANR rate, crash rate, excessive wakeups, and stuck partial wakelock metrics aggregated from production users. Ignoring Vitals during QA means the team has no pre-release benchmark to compare against and no early warning of Play Store demotion.

mobile-qa.title: Mobile Accessibility Testing
mobile-qa.description: Test mobile apps for accessibility compliance using screen readers and automated scanners. Ensure all interactive elements are reachable and describable.
topics: TalkBack and VoiceOver screen reader testing
mobile-qa.challenge: Perform a TalkBack accessibility audit of a sample Android app, document all accessibility barriers found, and prioritize by WCAG criterion.

children.title: TalkBack for Android Accessibility
children.description: TalkBack is the built-in Android screen reader used by millions of visually impaired users; it announces every element by its content description or class name. An app without content descriptions for images and custom views is completely silent for TalkBack users, making core features inaccessible and violating WCAG 1.1.1.

children.title: VoiceOver for iOS Accessibility
children.description: VoiceOver reads UIKit and SwiftUI accessibility labels and traits to navigate an iOS app entirely by audio and gesture. Elements that lack accessibility labels or have incorrect traits cause VoiceOver users to hear useless class names or skip interactive controls entirely.

children.title: Android Accessibility Scanner
children.description: Accessibility Scanner analyzes a running Android app and reports missing content descriptions, small touch targets, and insufficient color contrast in seconds. Running it manually is faster than writing test cases for each screen, and its findings map directly to WCAG success criteria.

children.title: Xcode Accessibility Inspector
children.description: Accessibility Inspector audits the accessibility tree of a running iOS app for label conflicts, missing traits, and contrast failures without requiring a physical device. Skipping this tool before release allows invisible accessibility barriers to reach users who file complaints or trigger legal action under ADA or EN 301 549.

children.title: Touch Target Size Requirements
children.description: WCAG 2.5.5 requires interactive touch targets to be at least 44x44 CSS pixels; Google recommends 48x48 dp. Targets smaller than this cause miss-taps that frustrate users with motor impairments and generate bug reports cataloged as UX issues rather than accessibility violations.

children.title: Color Contrast for Mobile Displays
children.description: Mobile screens are used outdoors in direct sunlight, which makes contrast issues far more disabling than on desktop. WCAG 1.4.3 requires a 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text; failing this on a mobile app alienates users in bright environments even if they have no visual impairment.

children.title: Accessibility Testing with Appium
children.description: Appium can query the accessibility tree and assert that content descriptions are populated and that focus order is logical for all interactive elements. Automating these checks catches regressions introduced by developers who add new screens without accessibility review.

children.title: Google Accessibility Test Framework
children.description: ATF integrates with Espresso to automatically check accessibility rules on every view rendered during a test run, without writing separate accessibility tests. Without ATF, accessibility coverage depends entirely on manual TalkBack sessions that are expensive to run on every PR.

mobile-qa.title: Page Object Model for Mobile Automation
mobile-qa.description: Apply Page Object Model and screen object patterns to your Appium tests to create a maintainable and scalable mobile test framework. Add reporting and parallel execution.
mobile-qa.challenge: Build a mobile test framework with screen objects, Pytest fixtures, parallel execution on 2 emulators, and Allure reporting with screenshots on failure.

children.title: Screen Object vs Page Object for Mobile
children.description: The Screen Object pattern adapts Page Object Model to the fact that mobile screens have gestures, platform-specific locators, and back-navigation that web pages do not. Applying a web POM verbatim to Appium produces one-size-fits-all locator methods that break on iOS-Android differences in element attribute naming.

children.title: BaseScreen and Shared Utilities
children.description: A BaseScreen class centralizes driver reference, wait helpers, gesture methods, and platform detection so all screen objects inherit them consistently. Without a BaseScreen, each screen object re-implements wait logic differently, creating a maintenance debt that grows with every new screen added.

children.title: Appium Fixtures with Pytest
children.description: Pytest fixtures manage Appium driver lifecycle including session start, test-level reset via app state clearing, and guaranteed teardown on failure. Without scoped fixtures, tests that fail mid-way leave sessions dangling and corrupt the state for subsequent tests in the suite.

children.title: Allure Reporting for Mobile Tests
children.description: Allure attaches screenshots, device logs, and test steps to every test result so failed tests are self-explanatory without local reproduction. Mobile test failures without attached screen captures require the engineer to re-run the test with a manually started recording tool, doubling diagnosis time.

children.title: pytest-xdist for Parallel Test Runs
children.description: pytest-xdist distributes tests across multiple worker processes, each owning a separate Appium session on a different emulator or device. Without it, a 50-test mobile suite that takes 2 minutes per test requires 100 minutes to complete sequentially, making it useless as a PR gate.

children.title: Test Data Management for Mobile
children.description: Mobile tests need isolated test accounts and fresh app state for each run because shared test data causes false failures when one test's writes corrupt another test's preconditions. Fixture-level account creation via API or app reset between tests is mandatory for stable parallel runs.

children.title: Headless Emulator Runs in CI
children.description: Headless Android emulators run on CI servers without a display server by using the -no-window flag, and AVD management must be scripted because GUI tools are unavailable. Teams that skip headless configuration cannot run Appium tests in GitHub Actions or any Linux-based CI runner.

children.title: Test Reporting and Artifact Storage
children.description: CI pipelines must store Allure reports, device logs, and screenshots as artifacts accessible after the runner terminates, otherwise failure evidence is lost when the container exits. Without artifact configuration, post-failure investigation requires re-running the suite which defeats the purpose of CI.

mobile-qa.title: CI/CD for Mobile and Release Automation
mobile-qa.description: Automate mobile build, sign, and test workflows in CI/CD pipelines so every commit triggers tests on real devices. Use Fastlane to manage app signing and deployment.
topics: Fastlane for iOS and Android automation
App distribution to testers with Fastlane
mobile-qa.challenge: Build a GitHub Actions pipeline that builds an Android APK, uploads it to BrowserStack, runs the full Appium suite on 3 real devices, and posts results.

children.title: Fastlane Lanes for Build and Test
children.description: Fastlane lanes encapsulate the multi-step build, sign, test, and distribute workflow in a single ruby DSL that runs identically on local machines and CI agents. Without Fastlane, each engineer and CI agent runs shell scripts that diverge over time, causing it-works-on-my-machine build failures.

children.title: Code Signing Automation with Fastlane Match
children.description: Fastlane Match syncs iOS provisioning profiles and certificates across team members and CI agents via an encrypted git repository. iOS builds fail with opaque codesign errors on any machine that lacks the correct certificate, and without Match this affects every new engineer and every new CI runner.

children.title: GitHub Actions Mobile Workflows
children.description: GitHub Actions provides macOS runners with Xcode pre-installed for iOS CI and Ubuntu runners with Android SDK for Android CI. Teams that do not configure these runners correctly run iOS tests on Linux (impossible) or skip Xcode version pinning and break when GitHub updates the runner image.

children.title: BrowserStack Integration in CI
children.description: BrowserStack CI integration involves uploading the app binary, passing the app URL as a capability, and collecting session results via the BrowserStack REST API all within a pipeline step. Doing this manually per run instead of automating it means engineers must remember to upload fresh builds before triggering tests, leading to stale-build false passes.

children.title: Firebase Crashlytics Integration
children.description: Firebase Crashlytics captures unhandled exceptions and non-fatal errors in production with symbolicated stack traces, device metadata, and breadcrumbs leading to the crash. Without Crashlytics or an equivalent, crashes discovered by users cannot be mapped to specific code lines and reproduce rates are unknown.

children.title: Sentry for Mobile Error Monitoring
children.description: Sentry provides real-time error alerting, release tracking, and performance monitoring for both Android and iOS with SDK-level integration. Teams that rely only on app store reviews learn about crash spikes days after they happen rather than receiving an alert within minutes of a bad deploy.

children.title: App Distribution to Testers
children.description: Fastlane Pilot (TestFlight) and Firebase App Distribution automate the distribution of pre-release builds to QA testers and stakeholders the moment a CI build passes. Manual distribution steps create delays, cause testers to test the wrong build version, and make it impossible to trace which build a bug was found on.

children.title: Mobile Test Results Dashboard
children.description: A test results dashboard aggregating pass rates, flakiness scores, and device-specific failure patterns across runs makes quality trends visible to the whole team. Without dashboards, test failures are noticed only when the pipeline blocks a merge rather than proactively during the development cycle.

api-qa.title: HTTP and REST API Fundamentals
api-qa.description: Build a solid understanding of how REST APIs work including HTTP methods, status codes, headers, and request/response bodies. Learn to read API documentation and send your first requests with Postman.
topics: HTTP methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH
HTTP status codes and their meaning
api-qa.challenge: Use Postman to interact with a public REST API, performing GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE operations and documenting each response.

children.title: HTTP Methods and Semantics
children.description: Each HTTP method carries a specific contract: GET is idempotent and safe, POST creates resources and is neither, PUT replaces entirely, PATCH modifies partially. Misunderstanding these semantics leads to tests that validate the wrong behavior and miss silent data mutations on the server.

children.title: Status Codes and Their Meaning
children.description: Status codes are the API's primary way of communicating success, client error, or server failure. A QA who treats any non-500 as a pass will miss business logic errors hidden in 200 responses with error payloads, and will fail to distinguish a 401 (unauthenticated) from a 403 (unauthorized).

children.title: Reading OpenAPI and Swagger Specs
children.description: OpenAPI specifications are the single source of truth for an API contract. Testers who cannot read them independently rely on developers to explain endpoints, which delays test coverage and causes misaligned expectations when the spec and implementation diverge.

children.title: REST Architectural Constraints
children.description: Understanding REST constraints such as statelessness, uniform interface, and client-server separation helps testers identify non-compliant behavior. An API that stores session state server-side violates REST principles and creates scalability bugs that only surface under load.

children.title: Headers and Content Negotiation
children.description: Request headers like Content-Type, Accept, and Authorization control how the server processes and responds to a request. Failing to test header variations means missing bugs where the API ignores content type, returns incorrect serialization formats, or silently accepts malformed auth.

children.title: JSON Body Validation
children.description: Response body shape is a contract: missing fields, wrong data types, or unexpected nulls break consumers silently. Testers must validate structure and types, not just status codes, because a backend can change a field from a string to an integer and return a 200 that still breaks the frontend.

children.title: Sending Requests in Postman
children.description: Postman is the entry point for exploratory API testing before automation. Not understanding Postman's request builder, authorization tabs, and response viewer means QA engineers must write code before they can test at all, dramatically slowing early feedback cycles.

children.title: Query Parameters and Path Variables
children.description: Query parameters filter and paginate resources while path variables identify them. Confusing the two leads to poorly designed test cases and missed bugs such as injection through unvalidated query params or broken pagination when limit and offset are mishandled.

api-qa.title: Postman Collections and Test Scripts
