children.title: 5 Whys and Fishbone Diagrams
children.description: Stopping root cause analysis at the first obvious cause means fixing symptoms rather than systems. The 5 Whys technique forces teams to drill through surface causes to the organisational or process-level origins where durable fixes live.

children.title: Incident Severity Classification
children.description: Without a shared severity matrix, incidents are triaged inconsistently — one team's P2 is another team's critical blocker. A published classification scheme aligns response urgency, communication protocols, and post-mortem requirements across the organisation.

children.title: QA Role in Production Incidents
children.description: QA teams that are uninvolved during production incidents miss critical information about real-world failure modes that should feed directly into test case design. QA's role is not to fix but to document, triage, and translate the incident into future test coverage.

children.title: Corrective Action Tracking
children.description: Post-mortems that produce action items with no owners or deadlines generate the illusion of improvement without actual change. Tracking corrective actions in Jira with explicit assignees and sprint commitments is the accountability mechanism that makes post-mortems worthwhile.

children.title: Using Monitoring Data to Improve Testing
children.description: Production monitoring surfaces user behaviour patterns and failure modes that testing environments never replicate. QA leads who consume observability data systematically improve their test strategies based on real usage rather than assumed usage.

children.title: Alerting and On-Call for QA Leads
children.description: QA leads who are not connected to production alerting will always be the last to know about quality failures that matter most. Establishing alerting on defect escape rate and user-impacting errors keeps quality leadership anchored in real-world outcomes.

qa-lead.title: Quality Culture and Continuous Improvement
qa-lead.description: Build a sustainable quality culture where quality is everyone responsibility, not just QA. Use continuous improvement frameworks to evolve your team processes over time.
topics: Quality culture vs quality control mindset
qa-lead.challenge: Design a quality champions program for a 20-person engineering team, including selection criteria, training plan, metrics, and quarterly review cadence.

children.title: Quality as Everyone's Responsibility
children.description: When quality is owned exclusively by a QA team, developers ship first and fix later, creating a permanent adversarial dynamic. Shifting to shared ownership requires changing incentives, ceremonies, and tools — not just mindset — and that change is the QA lead's most strategic work.

children.title: Quality Champions Programme
children.description: A centralised QA team cannot provide quality coverage for every squad in a scaled organisation. Quality champions — engineers embedded in feature teams with quality advocacy training — extend QA reach without proportional headcount growth.

children.title: PDCA Cycle for Process Improvement
children.description: Quality improvements applied without measurement produce unmeasurable results that cannot be repeated or defended. The Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle ensures every process change is hypothesised, tested, measured, and either adopted or discarded based on evidence.

children.title: Retrospective Formats for Quality
children.description: Using the same retrospective format every sprint produces diminishing returns as teams optimise for the format rather than genuine reflection. Rotating formats like 4Ls, Start-Stop-Continue, and timeline retrospectives surfaces different angles on quality debt.

children.title: Documentation Culture and Knowledge Sharing
children.description: Teams that only hold knowledge in people's heads suffer disproportionately when engineers leave or rotate. A documentation-first culture where test strategies, runbooks, and lessons learned are written down transforms individual expertise into organisational resilience.

resources.label: Building a Knowledge Base for QA

children.title: Quality OKRs and Quarterly Reviews
children.description: Annual quality goals reviewed once lose relevance to the fast-moving sprint cycle. Quarterly OKR reviews tied to retrospective findings keep quality objectives current and give the team regular opportunities to see their improvement work translated into outcomes.

children.title: Psychological Safety and Quality Reporting
children.description: In cultures where delivering bad quality news leads to blame, QA engineers self-censor and surface defects late or not at all. Psychological safety is therefore a direct quality enabler: the more safe it is to report problems early, the earlier problems are reported.

children.title: Lean Thinking in QA
children.description: Lean principles applied to QA — eliminating waiting time, reducing batch size, and exposing bottlenecks — produce faster feedback and less wasted testing effort. Waste in the QA process is a quality risk in itself: it delays defect detection.

resources.label: The Lean Startup Applied to QA

qa-lead.title: QA Program Ownership and Strategic Leadership
qa-lead.description: Own the QA program at an organizational level by setting multi-quarter strategy, managing budgets, building the team roadmap, and representing quality at the leadership table.
topics: Building a testing center of excellence
Reporting quality health to C-level executives
qa-lead.challenge: Build a full QA program proposal for a growing startup including team structure, tool stack, automation strategy, metrics framework, and 12-month roadmap.

children.title: Annual QA Roadmap Planning
children.description: QA organisations without a multi-quarter roadmap react to requests rather than investing strategically, producing a perpetual backlog of tooling debt, coverage gaps, and under-trained teams. A roadmap makes quality investment legible and defensible to leadership.

children.title: Building a Testing Centre of Excellence
children.description: Without a centre of excellence, automation frameworks multiply inconsistently across teams, creating incompatible toolchains that are impossible to maintain centrally. A CoE provides shared standards, reusable components, and governance without becoming a bottleneck.

children.title: Headcount Planning and Budget Management
children.description: QA leads who cannot translate quality risk into staffing requirements will always lose headcount battles to engineering teams that frame their needs in delivery terms. Budget justification requires connecting team capacity to business outcomes, not test counts.

children.title: QA Tooling Evaluation and Procurement
children.description: Choosing tools by popularity rather than by fit for your organisation's technical stack and team skills creates adoption failure that wastes both budget and implementation time. A structured evaluation framework covering capability, cost, and maintainability prevents tool sprawl.

children.title: Reporting Quality Health to C-Level
children.description: C-level executives need to understand quality as a business risk, not a technical metric. QA leads who frame quality reports in terms of customer impact, revenue risk, and time-to-market gain a seat at strategic conversations where quality investment decisions are made.

children.title: QA Team Brand and Recognition
children.description: QA teams that are invisible beyond their defect reports are the first to face budget cuts when engineering headcount is rationalised. Building a visible team brand through internal talks, documentation, and quality advocacy turns QA from a cost centre into a recognisable capability.

children.title: Scaling QA in Agile and DevOps Organisations
children.description: Practices that work for a three-person QA team break down at thirty engineers across multiple squads. Scaling quality requires embedding quality thinking into engineering workflows rather than scaling the central QA team proportionally.

children.title: Quality in Platform and DevOps Teams
children.description: Platform teams that build shared infrastructure without quality feedback loops ship internal tooling that blocks dependent product teams. QA program ownership at scale means ensuring quality thinking is embedded in platform teams, not just product squads.
